coredata.dc:description: A two-step pyrolysis process was successfully employed to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), a corrosion inhibitor whose structure and properties were deeply studied. The results indicate that the nitrogen in the obtained N-CDs consists of pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen, along with abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. This composition enables the chemical adsorption capabilities of N-CDs while also enhancing effective physical adsorption capacity. The corrosion inhibition performance of N-CDs was evaluated at concentrations ranging from 5 to 80 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency increasing with concentration and reaching over 95.52 % at a dosage of 80 mg/L. N-CDs adsorption followed the R-P (Redlich-Peterson) isotherm. A dense protective film is formed on the metal surface through both chemical and physical adsorption, facilitating this inhibition mechanism, and the inherent negative charge of N-CDs further enhances this effect. This study provides new insights into the development of more efficient corrosion inhibitors based on carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Novel hydroxyl and carboxyl group-enriched nitrogen-doped carbon dots N-CDs as highly efficient corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel Q235 in 1 M HCl

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A two-step pyrolysis process was successfully employed to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), a corrosion inhibitor whose structure and properties were deeply studied. The results indicate that the nitrogen in the obtained N-CDs consists of pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen, along with abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. This composition enables the chemical adsorption capabilities of N-CDs while also enhancing effective physical adsorption capacity. The corrosion inhibition performance of N-CDs was evaluated at concentrations ranging from 5 to 80 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency increasing with concentration and reaching over 95.52 % at a dosage of 80 mg/L. N-CDs adsorption followed the R-P (Redlich-Peterson) isotherm. A dense protective film is formed on the metal surface through both chemical and physical adsorption, facilitating this inhibition mechanism, and the inherent negative charge of N-CDs further enhances this effect. This study provides new insights into the development of more efficient corrosion inhibitors based on carbon dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is widely used across various industries due to its low cost and excellent mechanical properties [1]. In the surface treatment and maintenance of carbon steel equipment, pickling is a common process aimed at removing oxide layers, rust, mill scale, and other contaminants from the metal surface using acidic solutions [2]. However, carbon steel is highly susceptible to corrosion in acidic environments, and the pickling process itself can exacerbate metal corrosion [3]. The addition of corrosion inhibitors to pickling solutions is one of the most commonly used methods to prevent metal corrosion [4]. Effective inhibitors typically contain heteroatoms such as N, O, and S, as well as aromatic or heterocyclic compounds with π-electrons [5]. These materials form protective barriers on the surface of carbon steel through physical adsorption (electrostatic interactions between the carbon steel and the inhibitor) and/or chemical adsorption (coordination bonds) [2,5].

In recent years, studies have shown that a series of organic corrosion inhibitors containing sulfur atoms and benzene rings exhibit excellent corrosion inhibition performance at low concentrations [6–8]. However, although they are more environmentally friendly compared to traditional inhibitors [9], their biodegradability still requires further evaluation Consequently, the development of eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors has become a research hotspot. Currently, widely studied green corrosion inhibitors include ionic solutions [10], plant extracts [11], and carbon dots (CDs) [12], each offering unique advantages in terms of performance and sustainability. Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with fluorescent properties. Due to their excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization, they have attracted significant attention in the field of metal corrosion protection [13–18]. In 2017, Cui et al. [19] first reported the application of carbon dots as corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in a 1 M HCl solution. Subsequently, Cui et al. [20] synthesized two unique nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine as precursor materials via a solvothermal method. These N-CDs, named p–CDs and o–CDs, demonstrated corrosion inhibition efficiencies of 88 % and 94 %, respectively, for Q235 steel at a concentration of 200 mg/L, highlighting the significant potential of N-CDs in the field of corrosion inhibitors. In subsequent studies, the synthesis methods and corrosion inhibition mechanisms of N-CDs were further explored. Zhu et al. [21] synthesized N-CDs using ethylenediamine and citric acid as precursors via a hydrothermal method. They found that the N-CDs formed a protective film on the metal surface through both physical adsorption and chemical adsorption, which inhibited electron transfer, prevented the evolution of cathodic hydrogen ions, and suppressed the dissolution of anodic metal. Cao et al. [22] synthesized N-CDs using urea and citric acid as precursors via a hydrothermal method and investigated their application as corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Their study revealed that nitrogen in the N-CDs exists in the forms of graphitic nitrogen, pyridinic nitrogen, and pyrrolic nitrogen. Among these, graphitic nitrogen is responsible for physical adsorption, while pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen facilitate chemical adsorption. Liu et al. [23] produced N-CDs as corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution using ammonium citrate as a precursor. According to their findings, the adsorption of N-CDs and the formation of coordination bonds occurred simultaneously, and this concurrent adsorption completely covered the metal surface, preventing contact with the corrosive solution.

Although significant progress has been made in the research of highly efficient N-CDs corrosion inhibitors, current studies are primarily focused on the chemical adsorption mechanism and enhancing chemical adsorption capabilities. In contrast, the exploration of the physical adsorption process has been relatively limited. This research bias has, to some extent, restricted a comprehensive understanding of the performance and potential applications of carbon dot-based corrosion inhibitors. It is currently believed that carboxyl, hydroxyl, and graphitic nitrogen groups on N-CDs are critical for achieving physical adsorption on metal surfaces [20–24]. Enhancing the physical adsorption capability while maintaining chemical adsorption performance can further improve the corrosion inhibition activity of N-CDs. Since the surface of iron in hydrochloric acid carries a positive charge, negatively charged chloride ions often act as bridges through electrostatic interactions, a phenomenon commonly observed in studies of the physical adsorption process of carbon dots [12,25,26]. Designing negatively charged N-CDs in hydrochloric acid solutions could compete with negative chloride ions, and ensure chemical adsorption while enhancing physical adsorption, thus improving their corrosion inhibition performance.

In this work, hydroxyl and carboxyl group-enriched N-CDs were synthesized using L-glutamic acid, aspartic acid, and NaOH as precursors. The structure of the obtained N-CDs was comprehensively characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Raman Spectroscopy, X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–vis) Spectroscopy, and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). Potentiodynamic polarization(PDP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and weight loss tests were conducted to examine the corrosion inhibition capabilities of N-CDs. A structure–activity relationship was established, and the corrosion inhibition mechanism was proposed through characterization and analysis of metal samples in different conditions.

L-glutamic acid and aspartic acid, both of analytical grade, were purchased from Macklin Inc. Sodium hydroxide, also of analytical grade, was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Tianjin. Q235 carbon steel samples were sourced from Shandong Shengxin Science and Technology Co., Ltd., which had the following composition (wt%): C 0.19, Si 0.31, Mn 0.45, P 0.02, S 0.03, and the remainder Fe. For electrochemical tests, Q235 samples were cut into 1 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm cubes and encapsulated in epoxy resin to expose only a 1 cm2 surface area for testing. For weight loss experiments, the Q235 samples were sized at 2.5 cm × 2.5 cm × 0.3 cm with a hole diameter of 0.3 cm. All samples were ultrasonically cleaned in industrial ethanol and polished using silicon carbide paper with grits ranging from 400 to 2000 before testing. The deionized water was produced in-house at the laboratory, while the hydrochloric acid solution was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory.

The synthesis is comparable to that described by Qiao et al. [27], where aspartic acid and glutamic acid serve as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, and NaOH as the catalyst in a two-step solid-phase pyrolysis process, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . L-glutamic acid, aspartic acid, and sodium hydroxide were mixed in a molar ratio of 6:3:1 in a beaker. After mixing, the beaker was heated in a hot air oven to 408 K for 35 min, all raw materials were completely molten, then the temperature was raised to 453 K for an additional 15 min, achieve cross-linked carbonization. After the heat treatment, the mixture was left to cool to room temperature on its own, and the yellow solid product was removed from the beaker. This solid was placed in a beaker with 100 mL of deionized water and stirred with a magnetic stirrer to dissolve it. The mixture was then filtered first using medium-speed filter paper and then a 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane. The filtered solution was placed in a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Daltons for 24 h dialysis, during which the deionized water was replaced every 6 h. Following dialysis, The solution was freeze-dried to obtain a white solid, which is the N-CDs.

The form, particle size distribution and lattice spacing of N-CDs were characterized utilizing XRD (Panalytical Empyrean, Netherlands) and TEM (FEI Talos F200X G2, USA). To better elucidate the fine structure of N-CDs, Raman spectroscopy (Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution, Japan) was conducted using a 532 nm laser wavelength to record data in the range of 1000–2000 cm−1, allowing for the analysis of the level of graphitization. The FTIR (Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS50, USA) utilized diffuse infrared technology to obtain spectra in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 and the data of 200–700 nm was recorded using UV–vis spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35, USA) for the analysis of functional groups of N-CDs. The fine structure of N-CDs was further examined by XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). The zeta potential of N-CDs in 1 M HCl solution was tested using the zeta potential analyzer (Brookhaven, USA).

Samples were submerged in 1 M HCl solution for 48 h, both in the presence and absence of various concentrations of N-CDs. A constant temperature of 298 K was maintained throughout the process, which was carried out in 100 mL double-walled beakers. After immersion, the Q235 samples were gently rinsed with deionized water to eliminate corrosion residues, followed by drying with cold air. The mass of the samples was recorded before and after submerged with a precision of 0.1 mg. Each test can be repeated three times, and the average value was calculated. Equations (1) and (2) are used to compute the corrosion rate (V) and the inhibition efficiency (IE w%), respectively [28]:
(1)

where W 0 and W represent the weight of the Q235 carbon steel before and after the weight loss test, respectively. A denotes the sample's surface area, t denotes the immersion period, and V 0 and V represent the sample's corrosion rates in a 1 M HCl solution without and with different N-CDs concentrations, respectively.

N-CDs were dissolved in a 1 M HCl solution to prepare corrosion inhibitor solutions at concentrations of 5, 10, 20, 40, and 80 mg/L. The measurements were conducted using a three-electrode setup on the ACS2350M electrochemical workstation (CORRTEST, China). The system components included a platinum sheet measuring 1.0 cm2 as the counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode, and a working electrode made of Q235 carbon steel with an exposed area of 1.0 cm2, covered in epoxy resin [29,30]. Prior to performing PDP and EIS measurements, a steady open-circuit potential (E OCP) was attained. The amplitude of the sinusoidal potential was set to 10 mV, and the frequency range for EIS measurements was between 105 Hz and 10-2 Hz, ZSimpWin software was utilized to evaluate the obtained data. CView2 software was used to assess the data after the polarization curves were acquired at EOCP ± 250 mV at a scan rate of 0.5 mV/s [28]. To guarantee the accuracy of the data, each electrochemical test was conducted three times.

After the weight loss test, the Q235 carbon steel samples were collected, cleaned with deionized water, and dried using cold air. The surface morphology of the carbon steel was then investigated using a scanning electron microscope (TESCAN MIRA LMS, Czech Republic). To ascertain the elemental composition of the metal surface under various conditions, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was employed.

Fig. 2 shows the results of the TEM characterization of the N-CDs. The TEM and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the N-CDs are presented in Fig. 2(a) and (b), respectively. The particle size distribution was determined by randomly selecting 100 N-CDs from the TEM images, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(a). The findings indicate that the N-CDs have an average particle size of 2.85 nm and a relatively uniform size range, mostly between 2 and 5 nm. Lattice fringes are clearly visible in the HRTEM image shown in Fig. 2(b). Software analysis revealed that the lattice spacing is 0.33 nm, which corresponds to the graphite (0 0 2) planes [31].

The XRD data, illustrated in Fig. 2(c), show a broad peak across the 2θ range of 10°-80°, typical for structures similar to graphene, particularly carbon dots. This broad peak is likely associated with the stacking of graphene layers, but due to small size and the presence of amorphous structures, long-range order is not pronounced. The center of the broad peak is around 25° in 2θ, which usually corresponds to the (0 0 2) planes of graphene. The XRD results indicate an interlayer spacing slightly larger than that of graphite, attributable to the amorphous characteristics of the N-CDs, making the peak very broad. In graphite, this peak is usually sharper and positioned around 26.5 degrees. The variation in peak width and position may be due to quantum size effects and/or oxidation causing changes in the interlayer spacing. Considering that this spacing is very similar to the (0 0 2) plane distance of graphite (0.34 nm), it suggests that the core of N-CDs is predominantly composed of highly conjugated sp2 structural domains [26], consistent with the HRTEM results.

The Raman spectroscopy results, presented in Fig. 2(d), show two peaks at roughly 1374 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands of the N-CDs, respectively. The vibrations of sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon inside the hexagonal graphite lattice produce these bands. The intensity ratio (I D/I G) of 0.85 reflects the degree of graphitization of the N-CDs [32]. The Raman spectroscopy results are in agreement with the TEM and XRD analyses.

In summary, the analyses indicate that the N-CDs are primarily composed of graphene fragments stacked in ordered and disordered arrangements. The dominant core structure features highly conjugated sp2 domains, and the overall morphology is quasi-spherical with a relatively uniform particle size.
The zeta potential of N-CDs in 1 M HCl solution is summarized in Table 1 , which shows the values at different concentrations of N-CDs.

According to Qiao et al. [27], carbon dots rich in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups exhibit negative zeta potentials. The zeta potential results suggest that the N-CDs likely possess numerous hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface.

The FTIR and UV–Vis spectroscopy results of N-CDs are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and 3(b), respectively. In the FTIR spectrum, peaks at 3342.51 cm−1 and 2962.54 cm−1 are attributed to O–H/N–H and C–H vibrations, respectively [33]. Peaks at 1718.51, 1537.22, 1400.26, and 1213.17 cm−1 correspond to vibrations of CO, CC, C–N, and C–O, respectively [34,35]. The UV–Vis spectrum of N-CDs in aqueous solution displays three absorption peaks at 237, 298, and 351 nm, attributed to π-π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds, n-π* transitions of CO bonds, and n-π* transitions of heterocyclic groups (CN and –NH2), respectively [36–38]. These results preliminarily confirm the successful doping of nitrogen into the carbon dots. To further investigate the fine structure and understand the valence states of elements on the surface of N-CDs, XPS was employed. The results are shown in Fig. 3(c)–(f). Fig. 3(c) displays the full XPS spectrum of the N-CDs, confirming the presence of O, N, and C elements, with the composition detailed in the inserted table, further confirming successful nitrogen doping. Fig. 3(d)-(f) present the high-resolution spectra for C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum can be resolved into three peaks at 288.1, 286.2, and 284.8 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–N, and CO bonds [39]. The N1s spectrum indicates that nitrogen primarily exists in the form of pyridinic nitrogen (399.9 eV) and also as graphitic nitrogen (402.1 eV) [38]. The O1s spectrum can be resolved into two peaks, caused by C–O (531.6 eV) and CO (533.2 eV) [36]. These thorough analyses offer a detailed evaluation into the structural, electronic, and chemical properties of the N-CDs, highlighting their potential utility in various applications, particularly where surface functionality and stability in acidic environments are crucial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the corrosion inhibition of carbon steel by N-CDs in 1 M HCl solution
Analysis of weight loss Measurement results
ce:section.ce:para: One common method for assessing the effectiveness of corrosion inhibitors is weight loss assessment. By measuring the mass loss of metal samples in a corrosive medium with different inhibitor concentrations, and controlling the temperature and duration of the corrosion reaction, the inhibitor's performance can be evaluated. Eqs. (1) and (2) are used to compute the V and the IE w%, respectively [28].

The corrosion inhibition effectiveness of various N-CDs concentrations in a 1 M HCl solution on Q235 carbon steel is displayed in Fig. 4 , along with the corrosion rates of Q235 carbon steel. Table 2 shows the average mass loss (ΔW), corrosion rate (V), and inhibition efficiency (IE W) for carbon steel at different N-CD concentrations. Table S1 contains all of the detailed information from the weight loss testing.

As illustrated in Table 2 and Fig. 4, it is evident that as the concentration of N-CDs increases, the IE W gradually increases, indicating that the corrosion of carbon steel is being inhibited and the corrosion rate is decreasing. When the N-CDs concentration is below 20 mg/L, there is a significant increase in IE W; at 40 mg/L, the increase in IE W slows down, and at 80 mg/L, the average IE W reaches a maximum of 92.69 %, with the average corrosion rate reduced to 1.9029 g·m−2·h−1, a decrease of 13.71 times compared to the untreated sample (26.0860 g·m−2·h−1). This indicates that N-CDs are effective corrosion inhibitors for Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution, and that the inhibition efficacy increases with concentration, although more slowly at higher concentrations.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the PDP data for Q235 carbon steel at 298 K in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentrations of N-CDs. It is possible that the addition of N-CDs has no effect on the cathodic reaction mechanism, as the cathodic segments of the PDP curves show parallel patterns and the anodic branches nearly coincide. However, the cathodic reaction is effectively inhibited, as evidenced by the significant decrease in corrosion current density [40]. Table 3 displays the Tafel slopes (b a and b c), corrosion potentials (E corr), and corrosion current densities (i corr) for the anodic and cathodic reactions, which were obtained by fitting the data using CView2 software in the Tafel region close to the corrosion potential. IE PDP was determined using Equation (3).
(3)

In the formula, where

are the corrosion rates without and with inhibitor, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 5(a) and Table 3, the results of PDP experiments conducted on Q235 steel in a 1 M HCl solution indicate that the i corr values gradually decrease with the addition of N-CDs, exhibiting a trend similar to that of the inhibition efficiency. Furthermore, the i corr values are inversely proportional to the inhibitor concentration, demonstrating that the increased concentration of the inhibitor significantly suppresses the corrosion of carbon steel [41]. The corrosion potential shifted to the left. Since the maximum shift in corrosion potential was 55.84 mV, which is less than 85 mV, it can be inferred that N-CDs act as mixed-type corrosion inhibitors [25,42]. In addition, after the addition of N-CDs, both anodic and cathodic potentials decreased, with the most significant change observed in the cathodic slope, which decreased from −72.888 mV to –23.187 mV. These findings indicate that N-CDs, as a mixed-type corrosion inhibitor, primarily inhibit corrosion by providing cathodic protection. At an N-CDs concentration of 80 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency reached a maximum of 95.52 % in the 1 M HCl solution. The results of the PDP tests are consistent with those of the weight loss experiments, both showing similar trends in inhibition efficiency.

EIS data, as depicted in Fig. 5(b–d), show that the addition of N-CDs to 1 M HCl solution at 298 K results in a noticeable augment in the radius of the capacitive loop in Nyquist plots for Q235 steel, which indicates the producing of a protective layer on the steel surface. This layer reduces the area of active sites exposed on the steel surface, thereby reducing the charge transfer rate during the corrosion process and effectively suppressing corrosion [43]. The radius of the capacitive loop enlarges as the concentration of N-CDs, but the rate of increase slows down after reaching 40 mg/L, suggesting a near-saturation adsorption of N-CDs on the electrode surface, leading to an almost complete coverage. The strength of the correlation between the corrosion resistance of the material and the size of the impedance modulus at low frequency (|Z| f = 0.01 Hz) is significant [12]. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the inclusion of N-CDs caused the |Z| value to increase 11.47 times, from 10.26 to 117.73. The phase angle plots in Fig. 5(d) demonstrate a decrease in phase angle with increasing concentration of N-CDs, from approximately −40° to −70°. After adding N-CDs, the phase angle broadens, appearing as an overlap of two phase angle peaks, which could be due to the adsorption and desorption of N-CDs at the metal/solution interface or related to the double-layer capacitance (C dl) [40,44].

To better understand the electrochemical impedance data, an equivalent circuit model as shown in Fig. 5(e) was used to fit the data. Circuit I in Fig. 5(e) [45] represents the fitting without N-CDs, while Circuit II represents the fitting with N-CDs. The components R s, R f, and R ct in the circuit represent the solution resistance, film resistance, and charge transfer resistance, respectively. Constant phase elements (CPE f and CPE dl) take the role of the ideal membrane capacitance (C f) and C dl in the circuit due to the non-ideal capacitive behavior caused by electrode heterogeneity. The values for CPE can be determined using Equation (4) [46].
(4)

Eq. (4) was used to determine the impedance function of the CPE. The variables Y 0, j, ω, and n represent the magnitude of the CPE, imaginary unit, angular frequency, and dispersion coefficient, respectively. When n is −1, 0, or 1, the CPE indicates an inductor, resistor, or capacitor, respectively. When n between is −1 and 1, it indicates non-ideal capacitive or inductive behavior. n can be understood as the surface heterogeneity index [47]. Equation (5) provides the formulas for calculating C f and C dl.

It is evident from the EIS data that there is a good correlation with the results from the weight loss experiments and PDP tests. Table 4 shows that the charge transfer resistance (R ct) increases significantly when N-CDs are added as corrosion inhibitors. The increase of R ct suggests a thicker adsorption film, indicating that N-CDs displace pre-adsorbed water molecules on the carbon steel surface, and form a denser adsorption layer. This consequently reduces C dl and enhances the inhibition of corrosion on the carbon steel surface [35]. In the case of adding N-CDs, the values of n 1 and n 2 are non-integers, indicating that the metal surface's electrochemical reactions exhibit non-ideal dielectric behavior in their unmodified state. The corrosion inhibitor is added, changing n 1 to 1, while n 2 remains a non-integer. This suggests that the inhibitor creates a protective film with ideal capacitive properties. However, non-ideal dielectric characteristic is still present at the interface between the protective film and the metal. The value of n can also be understood as a measure of the surface roughness of the working electrode generally, a higher n value corresponds to a smoother surface. Therefore, the changes in n values in the table can be understood as the formation of a film by N-CDs on the metal surface, resulting in a smoother metal/solution interface compared to the surface without N-CDs. Furthermore, the resistance at the solution/film interface (R f), the resistance at the film/metal interface (R ct), and the total resistance obtained by summing these resistances (R p) are also considered. The maximum R p value, observed at a concentration of 80 mg/L, can be interpreted as the condition under which N-CDs adsorb most tightly to the metal surface, exhibiting the strongest interaction and thus forming the most dense and intact protective film. At this concentration, the highest inhibition efficiency is achieved, reaching 92.07 %.

The weight loss test, PDP, and EIS findings all show a high degree of agreement, indicating that N-CDs have a remarkable protective effect on Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. Notably, all corrosion inhibition capacity tests indicate that as N-CD concentration in 1 M HCl reaches 40 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency surpasses 90 %. This suggests that even at lower concentrations, N-CDs can effectively protect Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation into the corrosion inhibition mechanism of N-CDs in 1 M HCl solution on carbon steel
Exploration of adsorption isotherms of N-CDs on carbon steel surface
ce:section.ce:para:
The methodology for exploring the adsorption isotherms follows the same procedures as previous work [45,47]. Integrating the findings from Cen et al. [48] and Wu et al. [49], the three-parameter R-P equation, which is more appropriate for describing the adsorption process of nanoparticles, was employed to fit the experimental data. Equations (8), (9), and (10) represent the Langmuir, Freundlich, and R-P equations, respectively [50].

q e: Adsorptive ability.
q m: Maximum adsorptive capacity, unit: mg/L.
K Langmuir: Langmuir adsorption equilibrium constant, unit: L/mg.
C e: Concentration at equilibrium, unit: mg/L.
K Freundlich: Freundlich adsorption equilibrium constant.
n: The adsorbent’s adsorptive strength.
A, B, α: The R-P model’s Parameters.

Under certain circumstances, the three-parameter R-P adsorption isotherm can be reduced to either the Langmuir or Freundlich model [49]. It combines features of the Langmuir and Freundlich models, allowing the R-P equation to provides a more precise description of the adsorption process on heterogeneous surfaces compared to the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms [49]. The R-P model was chosen to fit the data primarily due to the aggregation tendency of nanoparticles and the microscopic heterogeneity of the metal surface. According to Cen et al. [48] and Wu et al. [49], The fitting accuracy of the linear exponential expression is superior to that of the linear logarithmic expression, transforming Eq. (10) into Eq. (11).

During the adsorption of nanoparticles, the α value can reflect the heterogeneity of the adsorption process. The α value gets closer to 1, it becomes increasingly similar to monolayer adsorption. When treating it as an idealized solid surface and neglecting the metal’s heterogeneity, the α value can be used to assess how nanoparticle aggregation affects the concentration of an effective corrosion inhibitor, the closer the α value is to 1, the lesser the impact. [47,48].

The adsorption amount q e is often deduced from the surface coverage rate θ. The formula for θ is given by Eq. (12).
(12)

IE

Eq. (5) can be used to derive the IE EIS in Eq. (12). Utilizing Excel and Origin software, the R-P equation fitting shows the highest goodness of fit (R 2 = 0.99993) when α is 1.01, as illustrated in Fig. 6 (a). An α value close to 1 suggests that the adsorption of N-CDs on the metal surface approximates an ideal monolayer adsorption, and the structural attributes and particle size of N-CDs have minimal impact on their effective corrosion inhibition concentration. On this basis, to acquire more detailed adsorption information, one can disregard the heterogeneity caused by self-aggregation and the minimal surface irregularities of the metal. The Langmuir isotherm equation is then employed for fitting. The results obtained from the Langmuir isotherm fitting, as shown in Fig. 6(b), exhibit an R 2 of 0.99991, which is slightly lower than that obtained from the R-P fitting. From this, two conclusions can be drawn: (1) The R-P equation is better suited than the Langmuir model for characterizing the adsorption of carbon dots corrosion inhibitors on metal surfaces. (2) It is inferred that the adsorption process of N-CDs on the metal surface closely approximates ideal monolayer adsorption. The K ads value reflects the sample's adsorption capacity; a higher K ads value indicates a stronger adsorption capacity. Additionally, the standard Gibbs free energy of adsorption

The K ads can be calculated using Equation (8). specifically K L from the equation (K ads = 0.82258 L/mg). The absolute temperature T in this investigation is 298 K, and the universal gas constant R is 8.314 J/(mol·K). After computation, the value of

is –33.75 kJ/mol, which falls between −20 and −40 kJ/mol. Consequently, it can be assumed that N-CDs use a mix of chemical and physical adsorption to adhere to the metal surface. This provides an essential basis for speculating about and examining the corrosion inhibition mechanism of N-CDs.

To more vividly observe and demonstrate the protective performance of N-CDs on Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl, samples of Q235 carbon steel immersed for 48 h in 1 M HCl solution with and without the addition of 80 mg/L N-CDs were taken. These samples were gently rinsed with deionized water and dried with cold air, then compared to an untested pristine sample of Q235 carbon steel using SEM and EDS analyses.

The surface of the pristine Q235 carbon steel, as shown in Fig. 7 (a) and (b), is relatively smooth with only scratches and occasional pits under 5 µm, likely from the polishing process. An EDS scan of the magnified image of the pristine Q235 carbon steel reveals its surface composition as shown in Fig. 7(c), where the main element detected is iron, with a carbon content of 9.55 at.%, and oxygen content only at 1.94 at.%.

After immersion in 1 M HCl without N-CDs, the surface morphology of Q235 carbon steel is displayed in Fig. 7(d). It is evident that the surface has undergone significant corrosion compared to the pristine state. A magnified view of the selected area in Fig. 7(d), shown in Fig. 7(e), clearly shows extensive surface undulations due to the presence of corrosion products. Subsequent EDS scanning of this area, as shown in Fig. 7(f), reveals a rapid decline in iron content, and an increase in carbon and oxygen contents to 20.34 at.% and 20.44 at.%, respectively. This is attributed to the corrosion of iron on the surface, resulting in its dissolution into the solution as iron ions, exposing the carbon anode and leading to the accumulation of oxidized corrosion products. Hence, a decrease in iron content and an increase in carbon and oxygen contents were observed.

For the Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl with 80 mg/L N-CDs, the surface shown in Fig. 7(g) appears to have a protective film, although not as smooth as the pristine sample, but considerably more uniform than the sample immersed without N-CDs. A magnified view of the selected area in Fig. 7(g), shown in Fig. 7(h), allows for clear observation of the film's layered structure, and the magnified image does not show the ravines and protrusions seen in Fig. 7(e). This indicates that the protective film formed by N-CDs on the Q235 surface effectively inhibits corrosion in 1 M HCl. The EDS scan of Fig. 7(h), shown in Fig. 7(i), supports this observation, with an increase in iron content compared to the sample immersed without N-CDs and reductions in carbon and oxygen contents to 16.41 at.% and 4.85 at.%, respectively. These findings show that N-CDs effectively protect Q235 carbon steel from corrosion in 1 M HCl. Compared to the original state, the carbon and oxygen content increased, likely due to the primary components of N-CDs being obtained mainly through pyrolytic oxidation. The EDS analysis confirms that N-CDs have formed a protective film on the Q235 carbon steel surface, further supporting the existence of the N-CDs corrosion prevention layer on the carbon steel surface [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of a corrosion inhibition model for N-CDs in 1 M HCl solution on Q235 carbon steel
ce:section.ce:para: As characterized in Section 3.3.2, the core of N-CDs predominantly consists of highly conjugated sp2 domains, enriched with nitrogen atoms bearing lone pairs of electrons. This configuration facilitates the chemical adsorption of N-CDs on the Q235 carbon steel surface. Results from Zeta potential, FTIR, and XPS analyses indicate that N-CDs are negatively charged in 1 M HCl solution and contain abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. These groups enable the development of a protective barrier on the Q235 carbon steel surface through physical adsorption. In the acidic environment, the positively charged surface of the carbon steel in the hydrochloric acid solution attracts the negatively charged chloride ions (Cl-) from the solution. N-CDs and Cl- ions compete with each other for adsorption sites on the metal surface. The electrostatic interactions facilitate the physical adsorption of N-CDs directly onto the Q235 carbon steel surface, thereby reducing the direct attack of chloride ions on the iron surface. An integrated analysis of electrochemical data and adsorption isotherm equations indicates that the corrosion inhibition by N-CDs is achieved through a synergistic effect of physical and chemical adsorption, forming a protective film on the Q235 carbon steel surface. This inhibits corrosion in the 1 M HCl solution. Surface analysis of Q235 carbon steel under different conditions further confirms this hypothesis. In summary, the proposed mechanism of corrosion inhibition is illustrated in Fig. 8 , which depicts how N-CDs interact with the metal surface and the corrosive environment to provide effective protection against corrosion.

As depicted in Fig. 8, Q235 carbon steel undergoes severe corrosion when immersed in 1 M HCl solution without the addition of N-CDs as corrosion inhibitors. However, the introduction of N-CDs into the 1 M HCl solution leads to the formation of a protective film on the surface of Q235 carbon steel, effectively inhibiting its corrosion. The combined actions of the physical and chemical adsorption of N-CDs result in the formation of this protective film. As illustrated on the right side of the figure, the blue lines indicate that some of the N-CDs are adsorbed onto the metal surface due to their inherent negative charge (physical adsorption). The brown lines represent some N-CDs whose heteroatoms, specifically nitrogen, form covalent or coordinate bonds with the vacant 3d orbitals on the metal surface (chemical adsorption). Through the combined effects of chemisorption and physisorption, N-CDs form a dense protective film on the metal surface, thereby mitigating corrosion.

The corrosion inhibition performance of N-CDs was examined through static weight loss tests, electrochemical measurements, and surface analysis. Based on the aforementioned findings, the following conclusions can be made:
1.
Hydroxyl and Carboxyl Group-Enriched Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots are efficient metal corrosion inhibitors.
2.
N-CDs can be effectively adsorbed to reduce corrosion at concentrations of 5–80 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency is as high as 96 % at a concentration of 80 mg/L.
3.
N-CDs were mixed-type inhibitors with predominant cathodic action.
4.
The R-P adsorption model is used to explain the adsorption behavior of N-CDs.
5.
N-CDs create a protective layer on metal surfaces through chemical and physical adsorption. Their negative charge in corrosive media strengthens interactions with the metal, enhancing film stability and corrosion resistance.

Although the application of CDs as a corrosion inhibitor has the advantage of green safety, it still needs a lot of research after its late start. In the future work, a detailed exploration of the forming theory of CDs plays an important role in the study of its modification and adsorption, and the industrial preparation of CDs is also a crucial part.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xuemei MaHer primary research interests include: Dr. Ma, a female of Han ethnicity born in 1979, is currently an Associate Professor and Master's Supervisor. She conducts in-depth research on analytical methods for active components in medicinal plants, the design of plant-based metal corrosion inhibitors, and the structure-activity relationships of surfactants. She has developed electrochemical sensors for ultrasensitive detection of flavonoids, alkaloids, and polyphenols, and designs green, efficient metal corrosion inhibitors using waste biomass. Dr. Ma has led or participated in several provincial and enterprise-funded projects and holds four national invention patents. Contact: maxuemei@nuc.edu.cn; 306933414@qq.com.
